title: 🎙️ Repair Culture & Practical Tech
description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today is about things you can fix, keep running, and pass on — repair culture, right-to-repair wins and setbacks, gear that outlives its manufacturer's support window, and the maker ethos of building to last rather than building to replace. We're interested in tools, devices, and projects where longevity, repairability, and self-sufficiency intersect, especially in rural and off-grid contexts where you can't just run to a repair shop.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Repair Culture & Practical Tech' — the right to repair movement, gear that outlasts its manufacturer, DIY fixes, and tools built for longevity.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder:
- 80-100: Directly about repairability, right-to-repair legislation wins or losses, teardowns scoring repairability (iFixit-style), gear designed for longevity or user serviceability, products outliving manufacturer support windows and how owners keep them running, permacomputing, vintage or legacy hardware still in active use, open-source hardware enabling long-term repairability, Louis Rossmann-style repair advocacy, 3D-printed replacement parts extending device life, battery replacement guides, rural or off-grid repair self-sufficiency.
- 55-79: Hands-on maker and DIY content (builds, modifications, hardware hacks, workshop projects), practical consumer electronics guides with real technical depth, 3D printing hardware and filament reviews, off-grid and solar power gear reviews, home lab hardware, tools and workshop equipment, battery and power tech — even without an explicit right-to-repair angle.
- 30-54: General gadget and device reviews or news with some hands-on or technical substance; consumer electronics that touch on software longevity or ecosystem lock-in; hardware products with open firmware or modular design as a selling point.
- 10-29: Surface-level product announcements, consumer gadget speculation without technical depth, leaked device specs, general tech news without a hardware or repair angle.
- 0-9: Geopolitical conflict, natural disaster (without engineering/preparedness angle), wildlife and conservation, working lands policy, Indigenous governance, science discoveries without DIY or hardware relevance, financial market news.

Strongly prefer: articles about the right-to-repair movement (legislation, court cases, company policies), repairability scores and teardowns, gear with documented longevity, open-source or user-serviceable hardware, stories of devices kept alive past manufacturer support end-of-life, and rural/off-grid self-sufficiency through repair. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Sun-like star caught after eating one of its own planets
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A distant Sun-like star appears to have devoured one of its planets, leaving behind a surprising chemical fingerprint. Researchers found an unusually high concentration of lithium, a strong sign that planetary material was mixed into the star. Careful comparisons with dozens of similar stars confirmed the signal is highly unusual, and scientists think a massive brown dwarf companion may have helped send the planet on its fatal plunge.
items.summary: A distant Sun-like star appears to have devoured one of its planets, leaving behind a surprising chemical fingerprint. Researchers found an unusually high concentration of lithium, a strong sign that planetary material was mixed into the star. Careful comparisons with dozens of similar stars

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Grace Episcopal Church of City Island in Bronx, New York
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Many New Yorkers will be familiar with Manhattan’s Grace Church, a French Gothic masterpiece built in 1846–1847 on Broadway. Or, if they’re from Brooklyn, perhaps they’re more acquainted with the Grace Church on Hicks Street, which was built in 1847 when the other Grace Church’s then-new location proved too far a trip for some faithful. However, for parishioners in the Bronx, specifically in the small seafaring community of City Point, Grace Episcopal Church on City Island Avenue is the one true bearer of the popular name – and this National Historic Place just so happens to have been built around the same time as its two more famous namesakes, in 1849.

Before the construction of the church, the Episcopal residents of City Island didn’t have much in the way of choices; Union Chapel on the corner of Tier and City Island Avenue was a Methodist institution. But that didn’t stop Reverend Cornelius Winter Bolton from offering afternoon services there every second Sunday of the month. Eventually he would move to Maryland and the services would stop, but he returned to lead the congregation, which was looking for a new location. Fortunately, his sister Adele had also taken an interest, and managed to raise money for the construction of a new church.

More of the family chipped in on designing elements of the new house of worship, with Cornelius’s brother John creating a stained glass work above the altar picturing ‘The Trial of Christ.’ (This would later be removed and then restored in 2016.) Finally, in keeping with its nautical surroundings, the building was constructed by local builders from the David Carll shipyard, in a style called “Carpenter Gothic,” reflected in the bold wooden rafters that soar over the nave.
items.summary: Many New Yorkers will be familiar with Manhattan’s Grace Church, a French Gothic masterpiece built in 1846–1847 on Broadway. Or, if they’re from Brooklyn, perhaps they’re more acquainted with the Grace Church on Hicks Street, which was built in 1847 when the other Grace Church’s then-new location

items.title: [Boing Boing] In 1978, a physicist took a particle accelerator beam through his brain and went back to work
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

On June 3, 1978, Soviet physicist Anatoli Bugorski was checking malfunctioning equipment on the U-70 synchrotron — the largest particle accelerator in the Soviet Union — when the safety mechanisms failed. He leaned over and put his head directly into the path of a 76 GeV proton beam. — Read the rest

The post In 1978, a physicist took a particle accelerator beam through his brain and went back to work appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] In the year 536, the sun dimmed for 18 months: "one of the worst periods to be alive"
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

In March of the year 536, the sky over Constantinople went dark for a year-and-a-half. The Roman historian Procopius wrote that "the sun gave forth its light without brightness… and it seemed exceedingly like the sun in eclipse." The statesman Cassiodorus described noon without shadows, a full moon "empty of splendour," and "a winter without storms, a spring without mildness, and a summer without heat." — Read the rest

The post In the year 536, the sun dimmed for 18 months: "one of the worst periods to be alive" appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] St. Stephen's Day Storm Memorial in Budapest, Hungary
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Budapest has had a range of controversial memorials erected in the last decade, some of which seem bent on rewriting the nation's history through somewhat rose-tinted glasses. This less-than-official-looking simple, unique and very colourful sign-cum-statue has until very recently been in a state of disrepair.
 
During the annual 20th August St. Stephen's Day celebrations, people line the Danube in central Budapest to remember the founding of the Hungarian state around 900 AD, to bless bread, and to enjoy a firework show above the spectacular architecture that lines the river.
 
In 2006, however, those celebrating along the river banks were in for a shock. A freak thunderstorm, along with 100 km winds, flooded the banks of the river and took the lives of three people, injuring at least 250 others. Since Budapest is a city that loves to memorialise...well, pretty much anything, a tree was erected to mark the spot on the river where those people lost their lives. Incredibly, that tree itself came down in a storm two years later, injuring pedestrians and resulting in two more deaths. The tree was then replaced with a more permanent memorial.
 
What currently marks the spot of the disaster is this less dangerous and wonderfully characterful plaque. The odd placement of the face atop the board bearing the description of the event and the rather folk-art approach, the signature style of its creator, Boldiszár Kö, make this plaque stand out against the muted colours of the classical architecture of Bem Quay, on the Buda side of the river.
items.summary: Budapest has had a range of controversial memorials erected in the last decade, some of which seem bent on rewriting the nation's history through somewhat rose-tinted glasses. This less-than-official-looking simple, unique and very colourful sign-cum-statue has until very recently been in a state

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Smygehuk Snake Barrier in Smygehamn, Sweden
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

[ NOTE TO THE EDITOR: If published, please add Smygehuk to the geo header to ensure the place gets included in the destination guide. ]

- - -

At Smygehuk, Sweden's southernmost point, a rust-colored barrier of steel and concrete separates a small fishing harbor from an adjacent nature reserve.

Winding 623 feet (190 meters) from the coastal road down to the Baltic Sea, the barrier is only about a foot (30 centimeters) high and sunk equally deep into the ground. Its outward-curving overhang stops European adders (Vipera berus), Sweden's only venomous snake, from climbing over.

The reserve is home to an isolated and scientifically significant adder population, monitored since 1981. As the colony grew, snakes increasingly wandered into cafés and other public spaces around the harbor.

After both a person and a dog were bitten, local authorities installed the barrier in 2019. Lethal control was never an option: all Swedish snake species are legally protected.

Evolutionary ecologist Thomas Madsen, who has studied the Smygehuk adders for decades, described the barrier as probably the only structure of its kind in the world.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists tested 39 sweeteners and found unexpected gut effects
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A large laboratory study found that many commonly used sweeteners can directly change the growth of gut bacteria. Researchers identified more than 100 cases in which sweeteners behaved differently when combined with medications, caffeine, or flavorings. The combination of isosteviol and the antidepressant duloxetine was especially disruptive, reducing beneficial bacteria and overall microbial diversity.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists built a camera that can track invisible particles in 3D
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

A new particle detector called PLATON could replace millions of tiny detector components with a single block of light-producing material. Using a light-field camera, highly sensitive photon sensors, and AI, it reconstructs particle paths in fast, detailed 3D. Simulations suggest it could match or surpass today’s best detectors while being far easier to scale. The technology may also lead to sharper PET medical scans.

items.title: [MacRumors] Apple Reportedly Ramps Up Vapor Chamber Orders for Foldable iPhone
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Apple has ramped up orders for vapor chamber cooling components said to be destined for its upcoming foldable iPhone and 20th-anniversary iPhone models, according to a Chinese leaker.


The Weibo account known as "Fixed Focus Digital" claims the increased order volume will first support the manufacture of Apple's book-style foldable, or "iPhone Ultra," which is expected to debut in September. Apple has reportedly asked suppliers to produce approximately 10 million foldable iPhones in 2026, up from an earlier forecast of seven to eight million units.

Fixed Focus Digital previously said Apple's first foldable iPhone would feature "impressive" vapor chamber cooling, claiming that the company is "going all out" with the device's thermal engineering. While there's no corroborating evidence, it's quite possible that the foldable design Apple is adopting could present unique cooling challenges because of its thinner internal structure and limited space for heat dissipation.

Apple introduced vapor chamber cooling to the iPhone lineup with last year's iPhone 17 Pro. The system uses a small amount of deionized water to move heat away from the A19 Pro chip and distribute it through the device's aluminum unibody frame.

Meanwhile, next year's 20th-anniversary iPhone will reportedly feature an edge-to-edge display with glass that curves around all four sides, with the aim of creating a nearly borderless appearance. If indeed we see such a device with a substantially redesigned enclosure, there's a good chance that it too will require a more advanced cooling system.

Apple is expected to offer two anniversary models in 2027 in sizes similar to the forthcoming iPhone 18 Pro and iPhone 18 Pro Max. The latter devices are rumored to retain the 6.3-inch and 6.9-inch dimensions of the current iPhone 17 Pro models, suggesting next year's commemorative lineup could use the same display sizes.

Earlier this week, Fixed Focus Digital claimed that Apple's "preferred approach" for the iPhone 20 is a return to glass, and that the relevant manufacturing facilities have already been renovated in advance of the project.
Tags: 20th-Anniversary iPhone, Fixed Focus Digital, Foldable iPhone

This article, "Apple Reportedly Ramps Up Vapor Chamber Orders for Foldable iPhone" first appeared on MacRumors.com

Discuss this article in our forums

The Weibo account known as " Fixed Focus Digital " claims the increased order volume will first support the

🔧 ✏️

After Trump reversed Biden-era incentives, 223 clean-energy programs were ended, resulting in $82.9 billion in investments lost.
items.summary: After Trump reversed Biden-era incentives, 223 clean-energy programs were ended, resulting in $82.9 billion in investments lost.
items._excerpt: After Trump reversed Biden-era incentives, 223 clean-energy programs were ended, resulting in $82.9 billion in investments lost.

items.title: [Pique Newsmagazine] Opinion: Wherever AI is heading next, older people want a say
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

The AI industry is dominated by young, male tech workers. It runs the risk of developing apps and tools that reproduce these gender and age-biases.
items.summary: The AI industry is dominated by young, male tech workers. It runs the risk of developing apps and tools that reproduce these gender and age-biases.

items.title: [Al Jazeera English] Iraq signs deals with Western oil firms, including to revive Syria pipeline
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

Deal with Chevron aims to rebuild a crude oil pipeline that runs from Iraq's oil-rich Kirkuk to Syria's Baniyas.
items.summary: Deal with Chevron aims to rebuild a crude oil pipeline that runs from Iraq's oil-rich Kirkuk to Syria's Baniyas.
items._excerpt: Deal with Chevron aims to rebuild a crude oil pipeline that runs from Iraq's oil-rich Kirkuk to Syria's Baniyas.

items.title: [TechCrunch] Period tracker Stardust shares users’ health data with analytics firm, says Mozilla research
items.content_html:
🔧 ✏️

One period tracker app tested by Mozilla was 'squeaky clean,' while another app was seen sharing users' health data with an analytics company, underscoring vast differences in user privacy among these apps.
items.summary: One period tracker app tested by Mozilla was 'squeaky clean,' while another app was seen sharing users' health data with an analytics company, underscoring vast differences in user privacy among these apps.
